More Notes: The paper identifies a 12.3% increase in obese adults with a 1°C increase in annual mean temperature as a significant impact of global warming. This is specifically found in temperate zones, not detailed on this page. The year provided reflects the publication year as the impact is a contemporary estimation.

The marginal effect of warming on obesity is higher in poorer countries compared to richer ones due to better adaptation capacities in wealthier nations. Using the 2050 estimates of warming.
A one-unit increase in log GDP per capita reduces the impact of warming on obesity. Using the 2050 estimates of warming.
The marginal effect of warming on obesity is higher in countries with older populations due to lower adaptation abilities. Using the 2050 estimates of warming.
The marginal effect of warming on obesity is lower in countries with younger populations. Using the 2050 estimates of warming.

Estimated threshold temperatures from the study: Long-run model suggests 18.5°C, Short-run model suggests 27.3°C. The impact is qualitatively described as initially increasing and then decreasing, centered around zero.

The projected impact estimates that global warming from 2016 to 2050 would result in 76.8 million more obese individuals in temperate zones.
The projected impact suggests 106.9 million fewer obese individuals in subtropical and tropical zones by 2050.
The increase is derived from a worldwide perspective, using data up to 2016, with variations across regions; tropical countries may not follow this trend.
